text: I'll start by reading the wiki config and scanning existing pages before creating the new wiki entry.

content:
1	# Second Brain Wiki Configuration
2	
3	This file configures the skill for Evan's environment. Edit paths and
4	projects as your work evolves.
5	
6	---
7	
8	## Paths
9	
10	| Path | Value | Description |
11	|------|-------|-------------|
12	| Wiki directory | `~/second-brain/wiki/` | Where wiki pages live |
13	| Sources directory | `~/second-brain/sources/` | Raw source files stored here |
14	| Inbox directory | `~/Desktop/Second Brain Inbox/` | Drop files here for processing |
15	
16	---
17	
18	## Active Projects
19	
20	These are your current projects and areas of focus. The skill uses these
21	to find connections when ingesting new content.
22	
23	- **evaluator-super-agent** — automated prototype quality assessment using persona-driven walkthroughs, 7-dimension rubric scoring, actor/evaluator separation
24	- **prototype-creator** — generates clickable prototypes from RFEs, tiered AC classification, Playwright automation
25	- **agent-eval-harness** — framework for evaluating AI skill quality, eval.yaml configs, judges, scoring pipelines
26	- **ai-sdlc-pipeline** — three-phase pipeline: RFE Creator (WHAT) → Prototype Creator (SHOW) → Strat Creator (HOW)
27	- **my-cursor-claw** — personal workspace with 20+ skills, MCP connections, notetaking, automations
28	- **decision-kit** — Decision Driven Development toolkit, /strategize, /decide, /journal
29	- **automated-usability-testing** — simulated usability testing tool, persona YAML files, evaluate-flow protocol
30	- **archie** — UX research agent for the RHOAIUX team
31	- **rhoai-prototypes** — RHOAI prototype collection for evaluation
32	
33	---
34	
35	## Calendar Integration
36	
37	| Setting | Value |
38	|---------|-------|
39	| Calendar name | `primary` |
40	| Auto-ingest meetings | `true` |
41	| Minimum meeting duration | `15 min` |
42	| Require notes/transcript | `true` |
43	| Skip recurring without notes | `true` |
44	
45	Events that qualify for auto-ingestion:
46	- Have attached meeting notes or a description with substance (not just "Weekly sync")
47	- Have a linked Google Doc (agenda, transcript, action items)
48	- Duration is 15+ minutes
49	- Are not recurring meetings that had no notes added
50	
51	---
52	
53	## MCP Integrations
54	
55	| Integration | MCP Server | Used for |
56	|-------------|-----------|----------|
57	| Google Docs | `user-google-workspace` | Extracting doc content |
58	| Google Calendar | `user-google-workspace` | Meeting ingestion |
59	| Slack | `project-0-my-cursor-claw-slack` | Channel message ingestion |
60	
61	---
62	
63	## Cross-Link Priority
64	
65	When multiple connections exist, prioritize these categories (from the
66	wiki index):
67	
68	1. **Red Hat AI-SDLC** — pipeline components, RFE/prototype/strat flow
69	2. **Evaluation & Quality** — rubrics, heuristics, eval harness
70	3. **Tools & Skills** — decision kit, cursor claw, skill overlays
71	4. **Meetings** — meeting notes with action items and decisions
72	5. **Standups** — sprint progress and blockers
73	6. **Session Logs** — what was built and shipped
74

1	# Wiki Page Template
2	
3	Use this template when creating new wiki pages. Copy the structure,
4	fill in the bracketed fields.
5	
6	---
7	
8	## Template
9	
10	```markdown
11	# <Page Title>
12	
13	<One-sentence summary of what this page covers.>
14	
15	## Key Points
16	
17	- <Bullet point 1: a concrete fact, concept, or takeaway>
18	- <Bullet point 2>
19	- <Bullet point 3>
20	- <Additional bullets as needed, max 10>
21	
22	## Related
23	
24	- [<Related Page Title>](<related-slug>.md) — <one-line reason for connection>
25	- [<Another Related Page>](<slug>.md) — <connection reason>
26	
27	## Sources
28	
29	- <path/to/source/file or URL where this came from>
30	- Ingested: <YYYY-MM-DD>
31	```
32	
33	---
34	
35	## Good Example
36	
37	```markdown
38	# Retrieval Augmented Generation
39	
40	Technique that grounds LLM responses in retrieved documents rather than
41	relying solely on training data.
42	
43	## Key Points
44	
45	- Combines a retriever (vector search) with a generator (LLM)
46	- Reduces hallucination by providing source documents as context
47	- Trade-off: retrieval quality limits generation quality
48	- Common stack: embeddings → vector DB → reranker → LLM prompt
49	- Works best when source corpus is well-chunked and up-to-date
50	
51	## Related
52	
53	- [Vector Databases](vector-databases.md) — the retrieval layer in RAG pipelines
54	- [Agent Eval Harness](agent-eval-harness.md) — uses embedding similarity for judge calibration
55	
56	## Sources
57	
58	- ~/second-brain/sources/what-is-rag.md
59	- Ingested: 2026-06-10
60	```
61	
62	---
63	
64	## Bad Example
65	
66	```markdown
67	# RAG
68	
69	Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) is a technique in artificial intelligence
70	that combines information retrieval with text generation. It was first proposed
71	by Lewis et al. in 2020. The basic idea is that instead of relying solely on
72	what a language model learned during training, you can augment it with relevant
73	documents retrieved at inference time.
74	
75	This approach has several advantages. First, it reduces hallucination because
76	the model can reference actual documents. Second, it allows the model to access
77	up-to-date information that wasn't in its training data. Third, it provides
78	transparency because you can trace responses back to source documents.
79	
80	There are several implementations of RAG available today...
81	```
82	
83	**Why it's bad:**
84	- Reads like a Wikipedia article, not a wiki node
85	- No bullet summaries (walls of prose)
86	- No Related section (isolated page, no connections)
87	- No Sources section (where did this come from?)
88	- Title is an acronym without expansion
89	- Contains filler ("There are several..." "This approach has...")
90

1	# Ansible AI Helpers Strategy — 2026-06-17
2	
3	Strategy session on integrating the UXD AI Helpers repo with Ansible's Carbonite harness. First major external consumer of the centralized skill distribution model.
4	
5	## Attendees
6	
7	Jeremy Perry, Mike Franke, Nicole Thoen, Jeff Puzzo, Cate Ferman, Leslie Hinson, Andy Braren, Melissa Grimes (invited: Taufique Rahman)
8	
9	## Context
10	
11	- Ansible uses **Carbonite** as their AI harness, managed by the **ATT team** (thinly veiled Star Wars reference)
12	- Jeff Puzzo and Nicole Thoen have been working with the Carbonite team on PatternFly integration — PatternFly is already in Carbonite's internal AI marketplace
13	- A readout with Ansible leadership the previous week surfaced interest in a **heuristic evaluation skill** as a concrete example of UX expertise
14	- Tracy (UXD leadership) advised the team to bring an opinionated point of view — not just "here are skills" but "here's how UXD recommends using them"
15	
16	## Key Decisions
17	
18	### 1. Heuristic Eval Selected as Pilot
19	The [heuristic evaluation skill](nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md) was selected as the initial pilot to demonstrate AI Helpers integration with Carbonite. Cate Ferman will coordinate a 2-3 session pilot with Ansible design and research teams to validate recommendations before broader rollout. Questions about guardrails, cadence, and usage guidelines are deferred until after pilot validation.
20	
21	### 2. Centralized Repo Integration via MRs
22	Nicole Thoen was firm: skills must flow through the AI Helpers repo via merge requests, not ad-hoc downloads. This preserves UXD ownership and distribution control. If teams pull skills locally and fork them, "they're not ours anymore." Jira tickets will track integration progress and give external teams visibility into timelines.
23	
24	### 3. June 30 Presentation to Ansible Leadership
25	Jeremy Perry secured a 30-minute slot with Kevin (Ansible technical leadership) and his staff. The pitch: UXD's opinionated design pipeline ("the purple box") — not just access to skills, but a recommended way of working. Key contacts on the Ansible side: Tim Puyer and Nathan Weatherly (global engineering working group).
26	
27	## The "Purple Box" Discussion
28	
29	Tracy raised an important framing question: teams shouldn't just get access to skills — they need UXD's point of view on how to use them effectively within their workflows. This is the [AI-SDLC Pipeline](ai-sdlc-pipeline.md) in practice. The challenge: each product team's orchestrator is different (Carbonite for Ansible, something else for OCP), so the mechanism for consuming skills varies even if the skills themselves are maintained centrally.
30	
31	## Scaling Concerns
32	
33	Nicole and Mike acknowledged the maintenance burden: per-orchestrator integration means each new product team is "square one" for wiring. OCP's Matt Carlton already reached out about getting skills into their AISDLC. Possible model: UXD people embedded in each product space own the orchestrator-specific integration while central team maintains the skills.
34	
35	Jeff proposed filling workflow gaps proactively — identify what Ansible's harness is missing, show up with a working solution, and create "I want that" moments rather than asking teams what they want.
36	
37	## Next Steps
38	
39	| Owner | Action |
40	|---|---|
41	| Melissa Grimes | Submit MR to add heuristic eval skill into AI Helpers repo |
42	| Jeff Puzzo | Configure technical connection between Carbonite and AI Helpers repo |
43	| Nicole Thoen | Create Jira tickets for AI Helpers integration (visibility for Ansible team) |
44	| Jeremy Perry + Mike Franke | Coordinate follow-up meeting next week |
45	| Cate Ferman + Nick Lemon | Prepare pilot plan presentation for Ansible team |
46	
47	## Internship Connection
48	
49	This meeting directly demonstrates the UXD skill distribution model that the [AI-SDLC Pipeline](ai-sdlc-pipeline.md) describes. The heuristic eval pilot connects to the same [Nielsen's Heuristics](nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md) methodology from Evan's first internship task. The [Agent Eval Harness](agent-eval-harness.md) patterns (eval.yaml, judges, automated scoring) will likely inform how pilot results are measured. The [Prototype Creator](prototype-creator.md) pipeline is a natural next candidate for Ansible integration after the heuristic eval pilot proves the distribution model.
50	
51	## Related
52	
53	- [AI-SDLC Pipeline](ai-sdlc-pipeline.md) — the "purple box" opinionated pipeline being pitched to Ansible
54	- [Nielsen's Heuristics & The Jagged Edge](nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md) — the heuristic eval skill selected as pilot
55	- [Agent Eval Harness](agent-eval-harness.md) — evaluation patterns relevant to pilot measurement
56	- [Prototype Creator](prototype-creator.md) — next candidate for Ansible integration
57	- [Evaluator Super Agent](evaluator-super-agent.md) — automated evaluation connects to the heuristic eval pilot
58	- [My Cursor Claw](my-cursor-claw.md) — the AI helpers repo and skills being distributed
59	- [WG: UXD Efficiency — June 29](wg-uxd-efficiency-2026-06-29.md) — continuation of Ansible integration work, AI helpers as primary vetted skills source
60	
61	## Sources
62	
63	- ~/Desktop/Notes Inbox/gemini-2026-06-17-sharing-ai-helpers-strategy-with-ansible---20260617-1329-edt---notes-by-gemini.txt
64

1	# Agent Eval Harness
2	
3	A framework for evaluating AI agent skill quality through automated testing, judge scoring, and iterative optimization.
4	
5	## How It Works
6	
7	1. **eval.yaml** — defines execution mode, dataset schema, output descriptions, judges, models, and thresholds
8	2. **Dataset** — test cases with realistic inputs for the skill
9	3. **Judges** — automated scorers that rate skill outputs against criteria
10	4. **Run** — executes the skill against test cases, scores with judges, reports results
11	5. **Optimize** — identifies failures, edits SKILL.md, re-runs to verify, checks for regressions
12	
13	## Key Skills (from the harness)
14	
15	- `/eval-analyze` — examine a skill and generate eval.yaml
16	- `/eval-dataset` — generate test cases for a skill
17	- `/eval-run` — execute evaluation against test cases
18	- `/eval-optimize` — automated improvement loop
19	- `/eval-review` — interactive human review of results
20	
21	## The Optimize Loop
22	
23	```
24	Run eval → Identify judge failures → Read traces → Edit SKILL.md → Re-run → Verify
25	```
26	
27	This is directly applicable to the [Evaluator Super Agent](evaluator-super-agent.md) work — the same pattern of rubric-based scoring, automated iteration, and quality gates.
28	
29	## MLflow Integration
30	
31	Results can be logged to MLflow for tracking, dataset syncing, and trace-level feedback.
32	
33	## Related
34	
35	- [Prototype Creator](prototype-creator.md) — uses rubric-based scoring for prototypes
36	- [UX Quality Rubric](ux-quality-rubric.md) — the 4-dimension scoring system
37	- [My Cursor Claw](my-cursor-claw.md) — includes run-evals skill
38	- [Evaluator Super Agent](evaluator-super-agent.md) — applies eval patterns to prototype evaluation
39	- [Red Hat AI-First Lessons](redhat-ai-first-lessons.md) — Jeremy Eder's presentation referencing EvalHub, Harbor, MLflow, and the harness
40	- [Ansible AI Helpers Strategy — 2026-06-17](ansible-ai-helpers-strategy-2026-06-17.md) — eval patterns relevant to measuring heuristic eval pilot results
41	- [Skill Overlays](skill-overlays.md) — overlays improve agent accuracy, directly relevant to eval quality
42	- [Prompt Engineering Best Practices](prompt-engineering-best-practices.md) — judge prompts and SKILL.md are prompt engineering artifacts; format specs and CoT improve scoring reliability
43	- [WG: UXD Efficiency — June 29](wg-uxd-efficiency-2026-06-29.md) — token efficiency and cost management for eval runs
44	
45	## Sources
46	
47	- sources/2026-06-04-agent-eval-harness-overview.html
48	- opendatahub-io agent-eval-harness skills (installed as plugins)
49

1	# Wiki Index
2	
3	A catalog of all wiki pages, organized by category.
4	
5	## Red Hat AI-SDLC
6	
7	- [Red Hat AI-First Lessons](redhat-ai-first-lessons.md) — Jeremy Eder's presentation on agentic SDLC, issue-to-PR bots, eval infrastructure, AgentReady, and culture shifts
8	- [Outcome Tiger Team](outcome-tiger-team.md) — proposal to formalize outcomes as the upstream step feeding the entire pipeline

11	- [Prototype Creator](prototype-creator.md) — Phase 2: generates clickable prototypes from RFEs
12	- [Prototype Creator Demo — May 14](prototype-creator-demo-may14.md) — full walkthrough of automated + human-in-the-loop workflows (Andy, Beau, Dash)

18	- [Vector Similarity Search](vector-similarity-search.md) — ANN algorithms (HNSW, IVF) for finding semantically similar content via embeddings

22	## Evaluation & Quality

26	- [Nielsen's Heuristics & The Jagged Edge](nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md) — AI model comparison using usability heuristics

31	- [My Cursor Claw](my-cursor-claw.md) — personal workspace with 20+ skills, 5 MCP connections, and automated daily review

41	- [RH AI-First UX — 2026-06-18](rh-ai-first-ux-2026-06-18.md) — Fable AI impressions, GitLab pages fix, prototype-evaluate demo, Outcome Creator project plan, Jira migration

45	- [AI-First Sync — 2026-06-25](ai-first-sync-2026-06-25.md) — Evan's prototype evaluator presentation, 3.6 branch, readiness check discussion
46	- [RH AI-First UX — 2026-06-25](rh-ai-first-ux-2026-06-25.md) — Outcome Creator JTBD integration, testing fragmentation, unified testing proposal
47	- [WG: UXD Efficiency — 2026-06-29](wg-uxd-efficiency-2026-06-29.md) — AI helpers repo strategy, Archie rebranding, productivity skills repo, AI cost management
48	- [Andy/Evan Meeting — 2026-06-29](andy-evan-2026-06-29.md) — evaluator progress, report UX, UXD PC7 cluster, sub-skill structure, Leaderboard of Pain
49	- [Midpoint Check-in — 2026-06-30](midpoint-checkin-2026-06-30.md) — Zack's strengths/opportunities feedback, energy management framework, career direction, report-as-product challenge
50	- [Weekly Design Sync — 2026-07-01](weekly-design-sync-2026-07-01.md) — evaluator demo prep, PatternFly 6 Q3 migration, Nielsen's heuristics as base rubric, scoring dimensions to revisit

52	## Standups

text: The thread content has LOW-MODERATE overlap with `ansible-ai-helpers-strategy-2026-06-17.md` (same centralized-repo theme) but adds new specifics: skill directory structure, independent versioning, and eval config policy. Creating a new page and cross-linking both directions.

input.content:
# Source: #uxd-rhoai Slack Thread — 2026-07-02

Channel: #uxd-rhoai
Thread started by Andy Braren, 2026-07-02

Andy: Hey team, quick update on the AI helpers repo strategy. We're going
with a centralized repo that distributes skills via MRs to consuming projects.
Each skill gets its own directory with a SKILL.md and references folder.

Evan: nice, does that mean we can version skills independently?

Andy: Exactly. Each skill directory is self-contained. Teams pull what they
need. We'll have a review process for new skills going in.

Zack: What about testing? Do we require eval configs for every skill?

Andy: Good question. Starting with recommended-but-optional. Once we have
enough examples of good eval.yaml configs, we'll make it required.

Evan: makes sense. the agent-eval-harness pattern would work well for that

Andy: Yep, that's the plan. I'll send a more detailed proposal next week.

content: File created successfully at: /Users/ejaquez/second-brain/sources/2026-07-02-uxd-rhoai-ai-helpers-slack.md (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

# AI Helpers Repo Strategy Update — 2026-07-02

Andy Braren's #uxd-rhoai Slack update confirming skill directory structure, independent versioning, and eval config policy for the centralized AI helpers repo.

## Key Points

- Centralized repo distributes skills to consuming projects via merge requests (MRs), not ad-hoc downloads
- Each skill lives in its own directory with a `SKILL.md` and a `references/` folder — fully self-contained
- Teams pull only the skills they need; independent versioning per skill directory
- Review process required for all new skills entering the repo
- Eval configs (`eval.yaml`) are **recommended-but-optional** now; will become **required** once enough good examples exist
- [Agent Eval Harness](agent-eval-harness.md) pattern identified as the right testing approach for skills
- Andy plans to send a more detailed proposal the following week (2026-07-08)

## Related

- [Ansible AI Helpers Strategy — 2026-06-17](ansible-ai-helpers-strategy-2026-06-17.md) — the earlier strategy meeting that established centralized repo distribution; this thread is a follow-up confirmation
- [Agent Eval Harness](agent-eval-harness.md) — the eval framework called out as the testing pattern for skills
- [WG: UXD Efficiency — 2026-06-29](wg-uxd-efficiency-2026-06-29.md) — AI helpers repo as primary vetted skills source, discussed the week before this thread
- [My Cursor Claw](my-cursor-claw.md) — example of a personal skills workspace that follows the self-contained skill directory pattern

## Sources

- ~/second-brain/sources/2026-07-02-uxd-rhoai-ai-helpers-slack.md
- Ingested: 2026-07-09

text:
Done. Here's what was created and linked:

**Page created:** `~/second-brain/wiki/ai-helpers-repo-slack-2026-07-02.md`

Key points captured:
- Centralized repo distributes skills via MRs (not ad-hoc)
- Each skill: own directory + `SKILL.md` + `references/` folder, self-contained
- Independent versioning per skill; teams pull only what they need
- Review process required for all new skills
- `eval.yaml` configs: recommended-but-optional now → required later
- Agent Eval Harness named as the testing pattern

**Cross-linked with:**
- `ansible-ai-helpers-strategy-2026-06-17.md` — the June 17 meeting that established the same centralized model; this thread is a follow-up confirmation
- `agent-eval-harness.md` — cited as the skill testing standard

**Connects to your active work:** This thread directly validates the **agent-eval-harness** project — Andy is codifying eval.yaml as the future-required testing standard, which means your work on that harness is becoming the canonical pattern for all UXD skills.
